title: Calamintha pseudosimensis
intro: Calamintha pseudosimensis, also known as dwarf calamintha or mountain basil, is a perennial herb belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae) that is native to the mountainous regions of Europe. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems, coughs, and colds, due to its antispasmodic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. Bioactive constituents of Calamintha pseudosimensis include essential oils such as borneol, bornyl acetate, and camphor, as well as flavonoids and phenolic acids like rosmarinic acid and ursolic acid. Calamintha pseudosimensis is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, often in combination with other herbs, to promote digestive health, relieve respiratory issues, and provide antioxidant benefits.
taxonomy: Calamintha pseudosimensis is a species of flowering plant in the mint family Lamiaceae. It belongs to the genus Calamintha, which is a group of small, shrubby herbs. Calamintha pseudosimensis is native to a specific region, and its classification is further divided into the kingdom Plantae and the phylum Angiospermophyta. This plant is closely related to other species within the Lamiaceae family, such as Calamintha siphylaea. The classification of Calamintha pseudosimensis is a part of the broader botanical classification system used to organize and identify plant species.
